scenes.title: Kubernetes
scenes.tagline: Declarative orchestration for containers, at scale
scenes.footnote: Go   ·   control plane + nodes   ·   reviewed past the happy path

beats.narration: This is Kubernetes -- the system that runs containers, in production, at scale.
You declare the state you want. Kubernetes' job, forever after, is to make reality match it -- and to keep it matching, through failure, scaling, and upgrade.
It is written in Go. We traced it through cmd and pkg -- and this time, deliberately, past the happy path, into where it strains.

scenes.heading: One front door, one source of truth

beats.narration: A Kubernetes cluster has a control plane -- a small set of components that together are its brain.

At the centre, the A-P-I server -- the only component anything else talks to, and the only one that touches storage. That is a deliberate choice: one front door makes authentication, admission, and audit uniform. The bill for it: the A-P-I server now sits on the critical path of everything.

Behind it, etcd -- and not merely 'a database'. A Raft cluster. It needs a quorum, a majority of an odd-numbered set, so it tolerates exactly (n minus one), over two, node failures -- and a network partition leaves the minority side unable to read or write. The whole control plane's availability is therefore bounded by etcd's.
The scheduler watches for pods that have no node yet, and binds each one -- by resource fit, by constraints, by spread.
And the controller-manager runs dozens of control loops -- one per kind of object. Hold on to that word: loops. It is the key to everything that follows.

nodes.label: Pods
nodes.sub: ≈110 per node, then it is full

beats.narration: Then the worker nodes -- the machines that actually run containers. Each runs two agents; and a node holds on the order of a hundred and ten pods before Kubernetes considers it full.
The kubelet is the node's agent. It does not poll the A-P-I server -- it holds a watch: a streamed, locally-cached mirror of just the pods bound to this node.
It runs no containers itself. It drives a container runtime -- containerd -- across a stable interface, the C-R-I.
kube-proxy programs the node's networking, so a Service -- a stable virtual address -- routes to whichever pods are healthy right now.
Both agents report status back, continuously, on that same stream. The control plane never asks 'what is running' -- it is told.

scenes.heading: You declare what, never how
scenes.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  replicas: 3            # the desired state: three of them
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: web }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: web }
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: nginx:1.27

beats.narration: Everything in Kubernetes is an object -- and you describe objects declaratively, almost always in YAML.
This is a Deployment. Notice what it never contains: the word how. It states a desired state, and asks the cluster to hold it true.
Three replicas. That number is a promise the cluster makes to you. Kill a pod, and a controller sees the count has fallen to two -- and creates a third, within seconds.
A template describes the pods -- their labels, their containers, the exact image. Nothing on this screen is a command. It is, top to bottom, a description.
Under every controller is one pattern: the reconciliation loop. It is the most important idea in Kubernetes.
The controller knows the desired state -- it watches the A-P-I server for the objects it owns.
It observes the actual state -- but not by asking. It reads an informer cache: a local mirror, fed by a watch stream. Fast to read -- and always very slightly behind reality.

It diffs, and it acts. And because two controllers may write the same object from slightly stale views, every write carries a resourceVersion -- optimistic concurrency. A conflicting write is rejected, and simply retried.

One subtlety decides the whole design: this loop is level-triggered. It re-reads the entire world every pass, instead of reacting only to change events. It is more work -- and it is exactly why a single missed event cannot break the cluster.
Watch a Deployment become running containers. You run kubectl apply.
The A-P-I server validates the objects and writes them to etcd. They now exist -- as desired state, and nothing more. Not one container is running yet.
A controller turns the Deployment into Pod objects. The scheduler, watching, sees three pods with no node.
The scheduler picks a node for each, and writes the binding back -- through the A-P-I server, of course. Nothing bypasses it.
On the chosen node, the kubelet is watching too. It sees a pod assigned to it.
The kubelet pulls the image and tells the runtime to start the container. And this is the step that most often fails in the real world -- a missing image, a registry rate-limit, a quota rejection.
It reports status back. Desired state is now actual state -- and every component reached that point by watching the A-P-I server. No direct calls, no central conductor.

nodes.label: Caches go stale
nodes.sub: controllers act on an old view

beats.narration: Every architecture buys something, and pays for it -- so here is Kubernetes' bill, the part a tour leaves out. Start with etcd. A network partition leaves the minority side unable to read or write, and there is a hard ceiling on the database, in the single-digit gigabytes. The cluster's availability simply is etcd's availability.

Reconciliation is not guaranteed to converge. A controller can wedge on a permanent error and retry forever under exponential backoff; two controllers can fight over one object; a pod can crash-loop, so reality never reaches the spec at all.
And every controller acts on a cache that is always a little behind. Usually harmless -- but occasionally a controller acts on a world that has already moved.
Now the deepest choice of all. Kubernetes is level-triggered -- each loop re-reads the entire world, from scratch, every pass.

It could have been edge-triggered -- act only on change events, far less work. But events can be dropped, and a dropped edge is a permanent mistake. So Kubernetes pays for resilience with constant, redundant re-reading. That is the trade -- and for a cluster, it is the right one.

scenes.heading: Kubernetes — an honest scorecard
points: Strong — declare the end state; the cluster converges, and self-heals.
Strong — one API, one source of truth: uniform auth, admission, audit.
Costly — the control plane's availability is bounded by an etcd quorum.
Costly — convergence is eventual; never instant, never fully guaranteed.
Skip it — for a handful of static services; the reconciliation tax only pays at scale.

beats.narration: The honest scorecard. In its favour: you declare the end state, and the cluster converges to it -- and re-converges when reality drifts. That is genuine self-healing.
And one A-P-I, one source of truth, means authentication, admission, and audit are uniform across the whole system.
Against it: the entire control plane's availability is bounded by an etcd quorum -- a hard, unavoidable limit.
And convergence is only eventual. Never instant -- and, as we saw, never fully guaranteed.

So do not reach for Kubernetes to run three static services -- the reconciliation tax is only worth paying at scale. Paid there, though, it buys a system that heals itself. That is the trade, named honestly.
